run.output:
[TIMING] decode: 0.1 ms
[TIMING] scan_filter: 89.4 ms
[TIMING] aggregation: 0.0 ms
[TIMING] sort: 0.0 ms
[TIMING] output: 0.2 ms
[TIMING] total: 164.4 ms
Query returned 4 rows

Q1.notes: Correct results. Performance is 1.9x slower than DuckDB (86.4ms) but 93x faster than PostgreSQL (15.0s). Primary bottleneck is scan_filter taking 54% of total time. Current implementation uses thread parallelism (64 threads) with morsel-driven execution and mmap I/O, but lacks SIMD vectorization, zone map utilization, and optimized filter ordering.

Q1.bottleneck: Scan-filter phase dominates with 89.4ms. The lineitem table scan processes 59.9M rows with a 98% selectivity filter (l_shipdate <= cutoff), touching 7 memory-mapped columns. Despite thread parallelism, the implementation uses scalar predicate evaluation, performs dictionary decoding before filtering, and doesn't leverage zone maps for block skipping.
Q1.root_cause: Multiple compounding inefficiencies: (1) Scalar filter evaluation instead of SIMD vectorization, (2) Dictionary decoding in hot loop before filter rejection, (3) Unused zone map index despite storage design specifying lineitem_shipdate_zonemap, (4) Per-row float conversions and arithmetic operations (division by 100.0), (5) Potential cache-line contention in hash table updates with 64 threads, (6) Non-optimal madvise strategy for HDD (MADV_SEQUENTIAL is acceptable but not ideal for random block skipping).

analysis.overall: Q1 iteration 0 achieves correct results with 164.4ms execution time, 1.9x slower than DuckDB target (86.4ms). The implementation already has strong foundations (thread parallelism, columnar mmap, dictionary encoding), but scan_filter dominates at 54% of runtime. Primary optimization opportunities: (1) SIMD vectorization for predicate evaluation, (2) Filter-first then decode pattern to avoid unnecessary dictionary lookups, (3) Zone map block skipping on sorted l_shipdate column, (4) Eliminate per-row float conversions by working in integer domain, (5) Optimize hash table layout and sizing for low-cardinality GROUP BY (only 4 groups).

performance_optimizations.description: Replace scalar predicate evaluation (line 172: `if (shipdate[i] <= cutoff_date)`) with AVX2/AVX-512 vectorized comparison. Current code processes one comparison per cycle; AVX2 can evaluate 8 int32 comparisons per instruction. With hardware supporting AVX-512 (per workload_analysis.json), this enables 16-way parallelism. Produce a selection vector (bitmask or indices) of qualifying rows, then apply dictionary decoding and aggregation only to matching rows. Expected speedup: 4-8x on filter evaluation portion. Implementation: Use _mm256_loadu_si256 to load 8 shipdate values, _mm256_set1_epi32(cutoff_date) for threshold broadcast, _mm256_cmple_epi32 for comparison, _mm256_movemask_ps to extract bitmask. Process in 256-row batches (fits in L1 cache). References: knowledge/parallelism/simd.md and query-execution/scan-filter-optimization.md
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 30-40ms reduction in scan_filter time (from 89.4ms to ~50-60ms). Filter evaluation is currently scalar; vectorizing 8-16 comparisons per instruction should yield ~5x speedup on the predicate evaluation portion. Total query time: 164ms → ~125-135ms (18-24% overall improvement).

performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation decodes dictionary codes for l_returnflag and l_linestatus (lines 174-175) for EVERY row before applying the date filter, which has 98% selectivity (keeps 98% of rows). For the 2% of rows that fail the filter, the dictionary decode is wasted work. Reorder operations: (1) Apply shipdate filter first using SIMD, (2) Produce selection vector of qualifying row indices, (3) Only decode l_returnflag/l_linestatus codes for rows in selection vector. This reduces dictionary lookups by 2% immediately. Additionally, consider late materialization: defer all column accesses (quantity, extendedprice, discount, tax) until after filtering. References: query-execution/scan-filter-optimization.md (predicate ordering section)
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10ms reduction. Dictionary decodes are cheap (array lookups), but eliminating 2% of them plus deferring 4 numeric column accesses saves cache bandwidth. The real benefit is cache efficiency — filtering first means subsequent operations work on a smaller working set. Total query time: ~125ms → ~115-120ms (cumulative 27-30% from baseline).

performance_optimizations.description: Storage design specifies a zone map index (lineitem_shipdate_zonemap) but the code doesn't load or use it. The lineitem table is sorted by l_shipdate (storage_design.json line 27: sort_order: ['l_shipdate']) with block_size=100000 rows. Each block has min/max shipdate metadata. For the query predicate l_shipdate <= 10471 (1998-09-02), load the zone map metadata file (likely tpch_sf10.gendb/lineitem_l_shipdate_zonemap.bin or metadata.json), check each block's max_shipdate, and skip entire 100K-row blocks where max_shipdate > 10471. With 98% selectivity, only ~2% of blocks can be fully skipped, but on HDD this saves expensive random seeks. Implementation: Before mmapping column files, parse zone map metadata, build a vector<size_t> of block indices to scan (skip_blocks = blocks where max > cutoff), then modify scan loop to process only included blocks. References: knowledge/indexing/zone-maps.md, storage/persistent-storage.md
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-8ms reduction on HDD. Zone maps are most effective for highly selective filters (Q6 with 1.9% selectivity would benefit more). Here, 98% selectivity means we can skip ~1.2M rows (2% of 59.9M) across ~12 blocks. On HDD, avoiding even a few blocks saves seek latency (5-10ms per seek). On SSD, benefit would be minimal (<2ms). Total time: ~115ms → ~107-112ms (cumulative 32-35% improvement).

performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation converts scaled int64_t values to double on EVERY row (lines 181-184: division by 100.0), performs floating-point arithmetic (lines 187-188), then applies Kahan summation. This is unnecessary: all TPC-H DECIMAL columns are stored as int64_t scaled by 100 (storage_design.json). Perform aggregation entirely in integer domain: sum_qty_scaled (int64), sum_price_scaled, sum_disc_price_scaled, sum_charge_scaled. Compute disc_price = price * (100 - disc) / 100 and charge = disc_price * (100 + tax) / 100 using integer multiply/divide. Only convert to double when outputting final results (divide by 100.0 once per group, not per row). This eliminates 4 FP divisions + 2 FP multiplies per row (59.9M rows × 6 FP ops = 359M FP ops). Integer multiply/divide is ~2x faster than FP on modern CPUs, and avoids Kahan compensation overhead (lines 191-195). References: storage design DECIMAL encoding, knowledge/query-execution/scan-filter-optimization.md
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15ms reduction. Eliminating ~359M floating-point operations and replacing with integer arithmetic yields significant savings. Kahan summation overhead also disappears (5 instructions per add → 1 instruction). Total time: ~110ms → ~95-100ms (cumulative 39-42% improvement, approaching DuckDB's 86.4ms target).

performance_optimizations.technique: Perfect Hash Aggregation for Low Cardinality
performance_optimizations.description: Current GROUP BY uses std::unordered_map<AggregateKey, AggregateValue> with custom hash function (lines 32-37) for keys (l_returnflag, l_linestatus). With only 3 distinct returnflag values (N, R, A) and 2 linestatus values (O, F), there are at most 6 possible groups (observed: 4 groups in results). Using a hash table with dynamic probing is overkill. Replace with perfect hashing: direct array indexing via agg_array[returnflag_code * 2 + linestatus_code] where returnflag_code ∈ [0,1,2] and linestatus_code ∈ [0,1]. This eliminates hash computation, probing, and key comparisons entirely — O(1) array access instead of O(1) expected hash lookup. Array size: 3 * 2 = 6 entries (24 bytes per AggregateValue × 6 = 144 bytes, fits in L1 cache). Thread-local arrays can be stack-allocated. References: knowledge/aggregation/hash-aggregation.md (perfect hashing section)
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-8ms reduction. Hash table operations (compute hash, probe, compare keys) are eliminated for 58.8M qualifying rows. Perfect hash is a single array index computation (multiply + add). Also improves cache locality (6-entry array vs. hash table with buckets). Total time: ~100ms → ~92-95ms (cumulative 42-44% improvement, matching or beating DuckDB's 108.8ms average, approaching 86.4ms minimum).

performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses madvise(ptr, size, MADV_SEQUENTIAL) on line 90, which hints sequential access. This is acceptable for full scans but doesn't optimize for the HDD hardware (workload_analysis.json: disk_type='hdd'). For HDD, random seeks are expensive (5-10ms latency), so we want to maximize sequential I/O within accessed blocks. With zone map block skipping (optimization #3), the access pattern becomes semi-random (skipping 2% of blocks). Consider: (1) Keep MADV_SEQUENTIAL for columns accessed sequentially (shipdate filter column), (2) For columns accessed after filtering (quantity, price, discount, tax, returnflag, linestatus), use MADV_WILLNEED *sparingly* only on HDD if zone map identifies large contiguous regions to skip — otherwise avoid prefetch on HDD to prevent wasted I/O. (3) Alternatively, use MADV_RANDOM for low-selectivity filters to signal the kernel not to over-prefetch. Note: On HDD with 98% selectivity, current MADV_SEQUENTIAL is likely optimal; this optimization is more relevant for Q6 (1.9% selectivity). For Q1, no change needed from current MADV_SEQUENTIAL. References: knowledge/storage/persistent-storage.md, io-libraries.md
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Negligible for Q1 (0-2ms) due to high selectivity. Current MADV_SEQUENTIAL is appropriate. Main benefit is avoiding performance regression if zone maps are added (don't combine aggressive prefetch with block skipping on HDD). For Q6, this matters more. Risk of premature optimization here.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reduce Thread Count / Increase Morsel Size
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation spawns 64 threads (std::thread::hardware_concurrency() on line 157), matching available cores. With 59.9M rows and morsel_size=100000 (line 158), there are ~600 total morsels. Each thread gets 600/64 ≈ 9.4 morsels initially, processing 100K rows per morsel. Potential issues: (1) Thread creation/join overhead for 64 threads (~0.5-1ms per thread × 64 = 32-64ms total, likely absorbed in scan_filter timing), (2) Cache-line false sharing in thread-local hash table updates (64 threads updating separate hash tables, then merging at lines 214-224). Consider: (1) Reduce thread count to 32-48 (still saturates CPU, reduces coordination overhead), (2) Increase morsel size to 256K-512K to improve cache locality within each morsel (current 100K × 7 columns × 4-8 bytes = 2.8-5.6 MB per morsel, exceeds L3 cache 44MB / 64 threads = 0.69 MB per thread). Optimal morsel size: L3_cache / num_threads / num_columns = 44MB / 32 / 7 ≈ 200K rows. References: knowledge/parallelism/thread-parallelism.md (morsel sizing)
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5ms reduction. Thread overhead and cache effects are secondary to SIMD/filter optimization. Tuning morsel size improves cache hit rate (100K → 200K keeps more data in L3). Reducing threads from 64 to 32-48 cuts thread creation overhead. Unlikely to yield major gains but worth testing after primary optimizations. Total time: ~92ms → ~87-90ms (cumulative 45-47% improvement).

summary: Q1 iteration 0: PASS with correct results, 164.4ms execution time (1.9x slower than DuckDB's 86.4ms target). Dominant bottleneck is scan_filter (89.4ms, 54% of total). Current implementation has solid foundations (64-thread parallelism, mmap columnar storage, dictionary encoding) but lacks critical optimizations. Top recommendations: (1) SIMD vectorized filtering (30-40ms gain), (2) Filter-before-decode pattern (5-10ms), (3) Zone map block skipping (3-8ms on HDD), (4) Integer-domain aggregation (10-15ms), (5) Perfect hash for 4 groups (5-8ms). Cumulative expected improvement: 53-81ms reduction → target 83-111ms total (matching or beating DuckDB's 108.8ms average). All optimizations are hardware-appropriate (HDD-aware I/O, AVX-512 SIMD available, 64 cores for parallelism). No critical fixes needed — correctness is validated.
